title: Setup Python Environment and Dependencies
description: Create virtual environment and install required packages for hierarchical Bayesian modeling, IRT, and evaluation

steps.step1: Create virtual environment: python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
steps.step2: Install PyTorch 2.1+ with CUDA 12.1: pip install torch torchvision torchaudio --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu121
steps.step3: Install Pyro for Bayesian inference: pip install pyro-ppl>=1.8.6
steps.step4: Install IRT utilities and scientific computing: pip install girth scipy scikit-learn pandas matplotlib seaborn tqdm

title: Collect and Preprocess Historical Evaluation Data
description: Download existing model evaluation results to train population model. Use pre-computed responses to avoid expensive LLM inference.

steps.step1: Download MMLU subject-level data: Use datasets library to load cais/mmlu with all 57 subjects (~14K items total)
steps.step2: Collect model evaluation data: Scrape HuggingFace Open LLM Leaderboard for 80-100 models with complete MMLU results
steps.step3: Extract metadata: family (llama2/3, qwen2/3, gemma, mistral, phi, falcon), parameter count, architecture (base/instruct/chat)
steps.step4: Simulate item responses: For each subject with n items and accuracy p, sample n Bernoulli(p) to create synthetic item-level data
steps.step5: Create train/test split: 60 models for population training, 20 held-out models for adaptive evaluation testing
steps.step6: Define 3 capability dimensions: STEM (14 subjects), Humanities (18 subjects), Applied (25 subjects)

title: Baseline: Random Item Selection
description: Simple baseline with random item selection and unidimensional IRT estimation

steps.step1: Implement RandomSelector: select_next_item() returns random choice from remaining items
steps.step2: Use 2PL IRT for ability estimation: P(correct) = sigmoid(a*(theta - b))
steps.step3: Run on 20 test models: Randomly select items, update theta via MAP estimation after each response
steps.step4: Track: prediction MAE vs items used, stop at 200 items or convergence
steps.step5: Run 3 seeds (42, 123, 456), record mean and std

title: Baseline: Independent MAT (Segall 1996 Style)
description: Classic multi-dimensional IRT with D-optimality selection, NO population structure. Directly addresses feedback to compare with established MAT.

steps.step1: Implement 3D MIRT: P(correct) = sigmoid(a1*theta1 + a2*theta2 + a3*theta3 - b)
steps.step2: Pre-calibrate item parameters using training models with marginal maximum likelihood
steps.step3: Implement D-optimality: Select item maximizing det(Fisher information matrix)
steps.step4: Initialize with flat prior N(0, I) for each model independently
steps.step5: Run variational inference for posterior updates after each response
steps.step6: Run on 20 test models with 3 seeds, track MAE vs items

title: Baseline: M3IRT-Style D-Optimality
description: Neural MIRT with D-optimality selection, adapted from Uebayashi et al. 2026. Tests whether neural IRT outperforms hierarchical Bayesian.

steps.step1: Implement neural MIRT: Item parameters a_i, b_i learned via neural network with embedding layer
steps.step2: Train neural IRT on training models: Optimize ELBO with Pyro, 2000 steps
steps.step3: Use D-optimality on learned Fisher information for item selection
steps.step4: Evaluate on test models with same protocol as other baselines

title: Baseline: Deep-CAT Style RL Selection
description: Deep reinforcement learning for item selection, adapted from Li et al. 2025 Deep-CAT. Tests whether learned RL policy outperforms information-theoretic selection.

steps.step1: Simplified Deep-CAT: Use policy gradient (REINFORCE) with small network
steps.step3: Train on 50 episodes using training models, early stopping
steps.step4: Use trained policy for item selection on test models

title: Baseline: Metadata Regression (Simple Zero-Shot)
description: Simple scaling law regression predicting performance from metadata. Tests if hierarchical model adds value beyond simple regression.

steps.step1: Train Ridge regression on training models: Features = [log(params), is_instruct, family_onehot]
steps.step2: Target = full-benchmark accuracy averaged across 57 subjects
steps.step3: Predict on 20 test models without observing any items (true zero-shot)
steps.step4: Compute Spearman correlation between predicted and true rankings

title: Phase 1: Train Hierarchical Population Model
description: Learn population structure - family distributions and covariances. Novel contribution: hierarchical Bayesian modeling for LLM population.

steps.step1: Define 3-level hierarchical model: Level1=theta_m, Level2=(mu_f, Sigma_f), Level3=hyperpriors
steps.step2: Model spec: theta_m ~ N(mu_f, Sigma_f); mu_f ~ N(mu_0, Sigma_0); Sigma_f ~ InverseWishart(Psi, nu)
steps.step3: Define 8 families: llama2, llama3, qwen2, qwen3, gemma, mistral, phi, other
steps.step4: Implement metadata network g_phi: MLP(input=4 [log_params, is_instruct, arch_code, family_emb], hidden=32, output=3)
steps.step5: Jointly optimize: (a) hierarchical parameters, (b) item parameters, (c) metadata network
steps.step6: Training: SVI with ClippedAdam, lr=0.01, 5000 steps, batch_size=10 models
steps.step7: Monitor ELBO convergence, verify monotonic decrease
steps.step8: Save: models/population_model.pt with learned mu_f, Sigma_f, g_phi weights, item parameters

title: Phase 2a: Zero-Shot Capability Prediction
description: Test zero-shot prediction using ONLY metadata and learned population structure. NO items observed.

steps.step1: For each test model: Extract metadata (family, params, architecture)
steps.step3: Sample ability: theta ~ N(mu_pred, Sigma_f) using learned family covariance
steps.step4: Predict performance on all 57 subjects without any observed responses
steps.step6: Compute Spearman correlation with true rankings across 20 test models

title: Phase 2b: Population-Aware Adaptive Evaluation
description: Main contribution: Adaptive evaluation with hierarchical information gain and population prior initialization.

steps.step1: Initialize with population prior: theta ~ N(g_phi(metadata), Sigma_f)
steps.step2: Implement Population EIG: Expected reduction in joint uncertainty H(theta, mu_f)
steps.step3: Use variational approximation for EIG to avoid expensive MC integration
steps.step4: Adaptive loop: Max 80 items or MAE<0.05 for each test model
steps.step5: At each step: compute EIG, select max, observe, update posterior
steps.step6: Track: items used, MAE, Kendall's tau

title: Phase 2c: Joint Multi-Model Evaluation
description: Test information sharing when evaluating 4 related models together. Novel: Cross-model information sharing via hierarchical posterior.

steps.step1: Select 4 models from same family with different sizes (e.g., qwen2-0.5B, 1.5B, 7B, 14B)
steps.step2: Baseline: Sum items needed when evaluating each independently (from Phase 2b results)
steps.step3: Joint evaluation: Share family parameters across all 4 models
steps.step4: Interleaved selection: Choose (model, item) pair with highest Population EIG
steps.step5: Track total items across all 4 models until all reach MAE<0.05

title: Ablation: Remove Population Prior
description: Test if population structure helps: Use flat prior N(0,I) instead of learned family prior.

steps.step1: PopBench-Flat: Same as Phase 2b but initialize theta ~ N(0, I)
steps.step2: Run on 20 test models with 3 seeds
steps.step3: Compare items to MAE<0.05: PopBench vs PopBench-Flat
steps.step4: Quantify benefit of population prior initialization

title: Ablation: Remove Metadata Network
description: Test if metadata conditioning helps: Use learned family mean instead of metadata-predicted mean.

steps.step1: PopBench-NoMeta: Use mu_f (learned family mean) for all models in family, ignoring individual metadata
steps.step2: Run zero-shot prediction on 20 test models
steps.step3: Compare Spearman: PopBench (with metadata) vs PopBench-NoMeta
steps.step4: Quantify benefit of scaling law information

title: Ablation: Standard vs Population EIG
description: Test if Population EIG improves over standard EIG (individual uncertainty only).

steps.step1: PopBench-StdEIG: Use standard EIG = reduction in H(theta_m) only, NOT joint uncertainty
steps.step3: Run on 20 test models with 3 seeds
steps.step4: Compare: Items to MAE<0.05, final prediction accuracy

title: Statistical Analysis and Success Verification
description: Comprehensive analysis verifying all success criteria with statistical tests.

steps.step1: Aggregate all results, compute mean and std across 3 seeds
steps.step2: Verify Criterion 1: Zero-shot Spearman > 0.7? Report with 95% CI
steps.step3: Verify Criterion 2: Items for MAE<0.05 < 10% of benchmark? MMLU=14K items, target <1400 items (expecting ~50-100)
steps.step4: Verify Criterion 3: Joint evaluation >30% item reduction?
steps.step5: Verify Criterion 4: Calibration - 90% credible intervals contain true value 85-95% of time
steps.step6: Paired t-tests: PopBench vs each baseline (alpha=0.05)
steps.step7: Effect sizes: Cohen's d for all comparisons

title: Generate Paper Figures
description: Create publication-quality visualizations showing key results.

steps.step1: Figure 1: Population structure - t-SNE of learned theta vectors colored by family
steps.step2: Figure 2: Learning curves - MAE vs items for all methods, shaded std
steps.step3: Figure 3: Zero-shot scatter - Predicted vs true accuracy, show Spearman rho
steps.step4: Figure 4: Joint evaluation - Bar chart: independent vs joint items
steps.step5: Figure 5: Scaling laws - Metadata predictions vs actual, R^2 value
steps.step6: Figure 6: Calibration - Coverage of credible intervals by method
steps.step7: Save to figures/ as PDF and PNG

title: Robustness and Sensitivity Analysis
description: Test robustness to distribution shift and hyperparameter choices.

steps.step1: Budget sensitivity: Evaluate with 20, 50, 100 item budgets
steps.step2: Test on 2 models from underrepresented families
